title: Psalms 123
subtitle: I Lift My Eyes to You

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Song of Ascent

sections.header: Verses 1–2 — “As the Eyes of Slaves Look to Their Master”

heb.paragraph: "I lift my eyes (our eyes, ʿēnēnū) to you, to you who sit enthroned in heaven. As the eyes of slaves look to the hand of their master, as the eyes of a female slave look to the hand of her mistress, so our eyes look to the LORD our God, till he shows us his mercy" (vv.1–2). The gaze psalm. Eyes fixed on God as a servant watches the master’s hand for the slightest signal. Complete attentiveness.

notes.note: "As the eyes of slaves look to the hand of their master, so our eyes look to the LORD our God." The analogy: servants watch the master’s hand for instruction, provision, or dismissal. The pilgrim watches God for mercy. MacArthur emphasizes the doctrinal significance of this passage within the framework of systematic theology. The text establishes principles that remain normative for the church — not merely descriptive of ancient Israel but prescriptive for how God's people should understand His character and respond in obedience.

refs.note: 'Our Father in heaven'—Jesus teaches disciples to lift eyes to the heavenly Father.
Set your hearts on things above, where Christ is seated at God's right hand.
My eyes are ever on the LORD, for only he will release my feet from the snare.

hist.context: Psalm 123 shifts the Ascent collection's focus upward: eyes lifted to the enthroned God. The servant/master and maid/mistress imagery portrays complete dependence—watching for the slightest gesture of favor. This posture of humble waiting defines Israel's stance during post-exilic humiliation under foreign powers. The psalm's brevity (4 verses) intensifies its plea.

sections.header: Verses 3–4 — “We Have Endured No End of Contempt”

heb.paragraph: "We have endured no end of contempt (būz). We have endured no end of ridicule from the arrogant, of contempt from the proud" (vv.3–4). The occasion for the upward gaze: suffering. The proud and arrogant mock; the pilgrims endure. The psalm is only four verses: two of watching, two of suffering. The watching is the response to the suffering.

refs.note: Nehemiah prays against the contempt of those mocking Jerusalem's rebuilding.
Paul finds strength in weakness, insults, and persecutions for Christ's sake.
If you are insulted for Christ's name, you are blessed.

hist.context: The community has endured 'more than enough' contempt from the proud and arrogant. This likely reflects post-exilic experience under Persian or Hellenistic rule, when returned exiles faced mockery from neighboring peoples and overlords. The repeated plea for mercy (three times in four verses) captures desperate dependence. The psalm ends without resolution—the community still waits for divine intervention.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: "As the eyes of slaves look to the hand of their master, so our eyes look to the LORD our God…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVWe have endured no end of ridicule from the arrogant, of contempt from the proud.ESVOur soul has had more than enough of the scorn of those who are at ease, of the contempt of the proud.NIVHave mercy on us, Lord, have mercy on us, for we have endured no end of contempt.ESVHave mercy upon us, O LORD, have mercy upon us, for we have had more than enough of contempt.

rec.text: ""As the eyes of slaves look to the hand of their master, so our eyes look to the LORD our God." The analogy: servants watch the master’s hand for instruction, provision, or dismissal. The pilgrim watc…"

rows.label: The Contempt — 123:3–4
rows.text: "No end of contempt." The arrogant mock. The pilgrim watches and endures. Four verses of focused faith.

hebtext.note: "I lift my eyes (our eyes, ʿēnēnū) to you, to you who sit enthroned in heaven. As the eyes of slaves look to the hand of their master, as the eyes of a female slave look to the hand of her mistress, so our eyes look to the LORD our God, till he shows us his mercy" (vv.1–2). The gaze psalm. Eyes fixed on God as a servant watches the master’s hand for the slightest signal. Complete attentiveness.
Replace 'būz' with 'bûz' and note that the psalm's structure involves more than just watching and suffering - it includes confident appeal to God based on covenant relationship.

themes.note: Faith and Mercy peak: fixed gaze on God amid contempt. Four verses of concentrated dependence.

prayer_prompt: The pilgrim lifts eyes to God, waiting for mercy like servants watching their master. Consider asking God about your posture of waiting — attentive, expectant, dependent.
